content:
Software Development

Software Engineering

JavaScript

React

Front End Development

8 min read
Just now

--

Patterns I’ve seen repeatedly after mentoring 500+ students — and what junior developers should do instead.*
Press enter or click to view image in full size

After mentoring more than 500 students and reviewing many junior frontend projects, I kept seeing the same mistakes appear again and again.
Most of them are not caused by a lack of intelligence or motivation.
They happen because beginners are often taught how to make an application work, but not how to think like an engineer.
Tutorials optimize for:
> “It runs.”
Real engineering asks:
> “Will it still work when the data, users, and requirements change?”

Here are the 10 mistakes I see junior frontend developers make most often.
1. Learning Frameworks Before JavaScript
Many juniors move to React before they are comfortable with JavaScript itself.
They can create a component, use hooks, and display data from an API, but they struggle to explain concepts such as:
- Closures
- Asynchronous behavior
- Object references
- Mutation
- Array methods
- Scope
- Why state sometimes appears stale
This creates a fragile kind of knowledge.
The developer can follow familiar React patterns, but the moment something behaves unexpectedly, they do not know where to start.
And in many cases, the unexpected behavior is not caused by React.
It is caused by a JavaScript concept they never fully understood.
React becomes much easier when you understand the language underneath it.
Do not rush through JavaScript just to reach a framework faster.
Learn the language deeply enough that you can explain what your code is doing — not only confirm that it works.

2. Copying Code Without Understanding It
Stack Overflow, tutorials, documentation, and AI tools make it easy to find working code within seconds.
That is useful.
The problem begins when developers copy a solution without understanding why it works.
It solves the immediate issue, so it feels productive.
But the cost appears later.
The code breaks in a slightly different situation.
The requirements change.
The developer needs to extend the feature.
Suddenly, the copied solution becomes difficult to modify because there is no mental model behind it.
The developer copied an answer, but not the method used to find the answer.
Before adding unfamiliar code to your project, read it line by line.
Ask:
- What does each part do?
- What problem is it solving?
- What would happen if I removed this line?
- Does this solution fit my application?
- Are there any security, performance, or maintenance risks?
Using existing solutions is normal.
Using code you cannot explain is dangerous.

3. Building Only Tutorial Projects
To-do lists, weather applications, calculators, and clones of popular websites are useful for learning syntax.
They help beginners practice components, state, API calls, forms, and routing.
But they rarely teach enough about the difficult parts of real engineering.
Tutorial projects are usually designed to work smoothly.
The data is clean.
The requirements are already known.
The correct architecture has already been chosen.
Someone else has removed most of the uncertainty before the tutorial begins.
Real projects are different.
APIs may return incomplete or inconsistent data.
Users may behave in unexpected ways.
Requirements may change halfway through development.
A decision that looked reasonable two weeks ago may suddenly become a problem.
You do not gain that experience by watching more tutorials.
You gain it by building something slightly more difficult than your current skill level.
Build a project where you have to make your own decisions.
Get stuck.
Research the problem.
Make mistakes.
Refactor your first solution.
That process teaches far more than copying another finished application step by step.

4. Ignoring Loading, Error, and Empty States
The happy path is the easiest part of most features.
The request succeeds.
The data arrives immediately.
The list contains items.
The user enters valid information.
The application behaves exactly as expected.
Junior projects often handle only this version of the feature.
Production applications cannot assume that everything will always work perfectly.
Requests take time.
Servers return errors.
Lists can be empty.
Users lose their internet connection.
Sessions expire.
A UI that only handles the happy path is not production-ready, because real users will eventually experience delays, empty results, and failed requests.
Every data-driven feature should consider at least these states:
- Loading
- Success
- Empty
- Error
In some cases, you may also need:
- Retry behavior
- Offline states
- Partial data
- Permission errors
- Session expiration
- Recovery after refreshing the page
These states are not optional details to add later.
They are part of the feature.

5. Putting Everything in Global State
Redux, Zustand, Context, and other state management tools can feel like one convenient solution for every type of state.
Once juniors learn one of these tools, they often begin placing everything inside it.
But most state does not need to be global.
A form input used by one component probably does not need to live in Redux.
A dropdown’s open or closed state probably does not need to be shared across the application.
A modal that belongs to one feature may not need a global store.
Overusing global state makes an application harder to understand.
Now a component can be affected by updates coming from anywhere in the codebase.
Debugging becomes more difficult.
Dependencies become less obvious.
Before deciding where state should live, ask:
“Who actually needs this state?”
If only one component needs it, keep it local.
If a small group of related components needs it, lift it to their nearest shared parent or use a focused context.
If the state represents server data, consider a server-state tool instead of manually copying everything into a global store.
Global state is useful.
But it should solve a real sharing problem — not become the default location for every value in the application.

6. Creating Components With Too Many Responsibilities
A component that handles data fetching, form logic, validation, business rules, state management, and rendering in one place is difficult to maintain.
This is common in junior codebases because keeping everything in one file initially feels simpler.
You can see the entire feature at once.
You do not need to decide where anything belongs.
But as the component grows, that simplicity disappears.
Large, mixed-responsibility components become:
- Harder to read
- Harder to test
- Harder to reuse
- Harder to debug
- Harder to change safely
- Harder for another developer to understand
The problem is not simply the number of lines.
A long component is not automatically bad.
The real problem is that unrelated responsibilities are mixed together.
Ask:
- Is this logic related to rendering?
- Could this behavior be moved into a custom hook?
- Is this section a separate reusable component?
- Does this business rule belong somewhere else?
- Can this part be tested independently?
Do not split components just to create more files.
Split them when doing so creates clearer responsibilities and makes the code easier to understand.

7. Debugging by Guessing Instead of Investigating
When something breaks, many juniors start changing code randomly until the error disappears.
They add `console.log` everywhere.
They remove parts of the component.
They reinstall packages.
They change multiple lines at once.
They copy another solution without first understanding the actual problem.
Sometimes this accidentally removes the visible error.
But it does not build real debugging skills.
A better debugging process is more structured.
Start by reproducing the problem consistently.
Then ask:
- What did I expect to happen?
- What actually happened?
- Where does the behavior first become incorrect?
- What input entered this function?
- What output came back?
- Did the network request succeed?
- What does the full error message say?
- Did the state change as expected?
Change one thing at a time.
Use browser developer tools.
Inspect network requests.
Read stack traces.
Check the data at each step.
Reduce the number of possible causes until only the real one remains.
Good debugging is not about guessing faster.
It is about investigating systematically.

8. Ignoring Accessibility and Mobile Users
Junior projects are often built and tested only in desktop Chrome, using a mouse, on a large screen.
That covers only one way real users interact with an application.
A feature is not complete simply because it looks correct on your laptop.
Can someone navigate it using only a keyboard?
Does a form input have a clear label?
Can a screen reader understand the page structure?
Is the text readable with sufficient contrast?
Does the layout work on a smaller screen?
Can users tap buttons comfortably on mobile?
Accessibility is not something to add at the end of a project.
A form that cannot be navigated with a keyboard is a broken form for users who rely on keyboard navigation.
A button without an understandable label is a broken button for someone using a screen reader.
The same applies to responsive design.
A layout that only works on a large desktop screen is not a finished layout.
Consider accessibility and mobile behavior while building the feature, not after everything else is complete.
It is usually cheaper and easier to design for these needs from the beginning than to fix them later.

9. Trusting AI-Generated Code Without Reviewing It
This is a newer mistake, but it is quickly becoming one of the most common.
AI tools can generate components, hooks, tests, API integrations, and validation logic within seconds.
That can make developers much faster.
But fast output is not always correct output.
AI-generated code may contain:
- Subtle bugs
- Security problems
- Outdated patterns
- Unnecessary complexity
- Incorrect assumptions
- Code that does not fit the existing architecture
- Solutions that work only for the happy path
The biggest risk appears when a developer accepts the result without understanding it.
If you do not understand what the AI wrote, you cannot confidently review it, debug it, or change it later.
Use AI to accelerate your work.
Ask it to explain unfamiliar code.
Compare different approaches.
Use it to create a first draft.
But review every line critically.
Treat AI-generated code the same way you would treat a pull request from a developer you do not know.
Because that is effectively what it is.
AI should improve your thinking — not replace it.

10. Trying to Learn Everything Instead of Building Deeply
Junior developers often feel pressure to learn everything at once.
React.
Next.js.
Vue.
Angular.
Redux.
GraphQL.
Node.js.
Docker.
Cloud platforms.
Testing tools.
System design.
Every week, another library or framework appears to become “essential.”
This creates developers who recognize many names but cannot build anything substantial with any of them.
Early in your career, depth is usually more valuable than breadth.
> Depth beats breadth early in a career.
Choose one stack.
Learn it properly.
Build a real project with it.
Take that project beyond the basic tutorial stage.
Add authentication.
Handle errors.
Build responsive layouts.
Write tests.
Improve performance.
Refactor weak decisions.
Deploy it.
Maintain it after the first version.
Let the problems you encounter guide what you learn next.
Do not learn tools simply because they are trending.
Learn them when they solve a problem you genuinely understand.

Final Thought
Junior developers do not need to know everything.
They need to understand what they are building, why they are building it that way, and what can go wrong.
Most mistakes on this list come from choosing something that works immediately over something that is properly understood.
The goal is not to write perfect code from the b
